# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What movie reveals something new every time it’s watched?

**Score:** 62
**Body:** Some films don’t just tell a story once they unfold again with each viewing. A detail appears, a line carries new meaning, or the whole mood shifts depending on the viewer. Which movies keep offering fresh discoveries no matter how many times they’re seen? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hot Fuzz

Every time I watch it I catch a new setup/payoff joke that I'd previously missed, just takes the film to a new level of a greater good
  - The whole Cornetto Trilogy, for that matter.
2. Memento (2000)
3. Most of the 70s-90s era slapstick movies, but principally *Airplane*.
4. The Big Lebowski
5. Synecdoche New York. So rich and dense it demands multiple viewings.
6. 2001: A Space Odyssey

Mulholland Drive

The Shining
7. Apocalypse Now


Every time I watch it it's a somewhat different movie.
8. Monty Python's The Holy Grail. 

Make sure to read the credits.
9. The Big Lebowski

## 2. Classic movie recommendations

**Score:** 51
**Body:** Hello! 

My daughters and I have started a Friday movie night where we get together and watch a classic movie. So far we’ve watched The Way We Where and National Velvet. 
Looking to get together a list so we can hopefully continue this movie night. 
We don’t want anything gory or stimulating. Would love to see what you guys recommend. 
Daughters are 19 and 20. Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. First of all, I love this! My parents raised me on classic movies. Since we’re coming up on Halloween here’s a few I would suggest:
- Hush, Hush, Sweet Charlotte
- What Ever Happened to Baby Jane
- Mommie Dearest
- Rebecca

Others I love and not mentioned yet:
- The Maltese Falcon
- Philadelphia Story
- Bringing Up Baby
- His Girl Friday
- The Women
- Auntie Mame
- The African Queen
- It Happened One Night
- Arsenic and Old Lace
- She Done Him Wrong
- A Place in the Sun
- Suddenly, Last Summer
- Cleopatra
- Annie Oakley
- My Fair Lady
- Jezebel
  - Bringing up baby is a great comedy
2. **All About Eve** (1950)
- Backstage story revolving around aspiring actress Eve Harrington (Anne Baxter). Tattered and forlorn, Eve shows up in the dressing room of Broadway mega-star Margo Channing (Bette Davis), telling a melancholy life story to Margo and her friends. Margo takes Eve under her wing, and it appears that Eve is a conniver that uses Margo.

**Mildred Pierce** (1945)
- When Mildred Pierce's (Joan Crawford) wealthy husband leaves her for another woman, Mildred decides to raise her two daughters on her own. Despite Mildred's financial successes in the restaurant business, her oldest daughter, Veda (Ann Blyth), resents her mother for degrading their social status. In the midst of a police investigation after the death of her second husband, Mildred must evaluate her own freedom and her complicated relationship with her daughter.

**Roman Holiday** (1953)
- Overwhelmed by her suffocating schedule, touring European princess Ann (Audrey Hepburn) takes off for a night while in Rome. When a sedative she took from her doctor kicks in, however, she falls asleep on a park bench and is found by an American reporter, Joe Bradley (Gregory Peck),who takes her back to his apartment for safety. At work the next morning, Joe finds out Ann's regal identity and bets his editor he can get exclusive interview with her, but romance soon gets in the way.
3. What about Terms of Endearment?
4. Splendor in the Grass, 
Rebel Without A Cause,
Casablanca,
Vertigo,
Rear Window,
A Summer Place,
Singin’ in the Rain
5. Meet me in St. Louis (1944)
6. Cat on a Hot Tin Roof, Imitation of Life
7. Auntie Mame (1958)! It’s funny and sweet and has incredible sets!
8. Roman Holiday, 12 Angry Men, White Christmas (in December)
9. Breakfast at Tiffany’s

I Married a Witch

Barefoot in the Park

High Society

## 3. What are the best movies about cults?

**Score:** 44
**Body:** Been reading Mindhunter lately and the part about Manson had me curious to watch some movies about cults - any suggestions welcome!

Edit: so many more suggestions than I expected - thanks all!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Midsommar

The Wicker Man

Martha Marcy May Marlene

The Sacrament
  - I was coming to suggest MMMM 

John Hawkes was amazing. He blew me away with the three films he made 

*Winter’s Bone* in 2010 with Jennifer Lawrence

*Martha Marcy May Marlene* in 2011

*The Sessions* in 2012 w/Helen Hunt & Wm. H Macy

Just one phenomenal role after another!
2. Eyes wide shut
3. The Endless (2017)
4. The Master
5. The Wicker Man (1973)

The endless

The ritual

The empty man

mother!

Hereditary

Midsommar 

Most of these have supernatural elements tho
6. Ninth gate
7. Midsommar and Eyes Wide Shut are probably the best of the bunch. The Sacrament and End of the Line are a fun watch, too.


Unfortunately, there aren't really any good non-documentary movies about Charles Manson. The only one worth watching is the 50 year old made for TV movie Helter Skelter. And it's a bit rough since its a bit boring and 3 hours long. It's also based off the Vincent Bugliosi book. And while Bugliosi's theory may have convicted Manson, it doesn't mean it's that accurate.
8. The Invitation

The Conspiracy
9. Passion of the Christ.

## 4. Action movies where lead character is not a gunslinger or trained professional

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I was watching National Treasure starring Nicholas Cage the other day and realised Nicholas cage is not a fighting back with martial arts and fire gun at the bad guys kinda guy. He was mostly running and saving himself by hiding. What are other cool movies like this


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Trouble in Little China
  - It’s a great watch when you realize Jack is the sidekick
2. The Fugitive (1993)
  - I didn't kill my wife. 


I don't care.
3. Pee-Wee's Big Adventure (1985)
4. Run Lola Run
5. Guns akimbo
6. Three Days of the Condor - A great Robert Redford movie.  He is a CIA analyst with no special training who has to go on the run.  He survives and unravels the mystery by using his intelligence and guile.


Nick of Time - It stars Johnny Depp and Christopher Walken.  Depp plays an ordinary guy whose daughter is kidnapped.  They threaten to kill her unless Depp assassinates a politician. It's very tense and engaging.
7. Blue Ruin
8. Hudson Hawk

## 5. Movies that consistently make you laugh?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Looking for movies that are your go-to on days where you need a good fun escape. Lots of laughs throughout, puts a smile on your face, etc.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Office Space
2. Airplane. Every time.
3. Young Frankenstein
4. Old School
5. KIngpin for the WIN
6. Old, but constantly hilarious: "It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World"
7. Clue

So I Married an Axe Murderer 

Groundhog Day

Scrooged
8. Mean Girls and Bridesmaids!
9. tropic thunder
10. Mel Brooks "Blazing Saddles".

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a couple more toxic than Edward and Bella from Twilight

**Score:** 152

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jacob and their daughter?
2. Mickey and Mallory in Natural Born Killers,
3. Jaime and Cersei Lannister
4. Joker and Harley Quinn
5. Tyler Durden and Marla Singer in Fight Club.  
6. Ike and Tina
7. Jacob and his child bride from Twilight
8. Christian Grey and Anastasia Steele from the Fifty Shades series
9. Gollum and Gollum
10. Mickey and Mallory from Natural Born Killers…and their antecedents Kit and Holly (Martin Sheen, Sissy Spacek) from Badlands (1973).

## 2. One Battle After Another is a Masterpiece

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Just got home from watching it. HELL YEAH

The best film since 2019 is the trio of Parasite/ Irishman and OUATIH. 

I do find it better than those 3, and in fact, Paul Thomas Anderson best film. Boogie Nights at 2 now. 

It has so much to say and just beautiful and tragic and hilarious and deep film. 

Leo ... is amazing. It's just amazing. I hope he gets his 2nd and well-deserved Oscar here. PTA better win, Director, and Picture here.. Brilliant stuff

I felt similar in theaters watching Tarantinos Inglorious Basterds, Scorsese with Departed and Wolf of Wall Street ( turned 18 that year of Departed, hence no Goodfellas), Spielberg as a 5 year old watching Jurassic Park, The Dark Knight for Nolan. I knew I was watching a masterpiece and or the should be best Pic winner. 

Would love to talk more about it but just wanted to post. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm glad! Going to see it in theaters soon. First movie in theaters since OUATIH for me.
  - Oh, then you're in for a treat if the last film in theaters was OUATIH! 

The performances in both films are on my  Mt Rushmore of Leo performances. 

Excited for you
2. watching it Sunday - so looking forward to it.
3. I saw both OBAA and Weapons on the same day. I of course can admit that Battle was the superior movie, but I've been thinking about Weapons more.
  - I can totally relate to that. I'm watching both films again Sunday with friends. Can't wait
4. Just saw it absolute masterpiece
5. agree
6. I’ve never gotten the DeCaprio hype but he’s pretty amazing in this.  Sean Penn is - it’s hard to describe - but it’s amazing. Del Toro as great as ever.
7. I saw it on Wednesday and loved it. Great film. I wasn't sure what to expect going in and it blew me away.
8. Just watched it and it lived up to like 80-90 percent of the hype and that’s saying a lot. Just imagine what you love about PTA and you’ll find it in this movie. 

## 3. Probably a perfect movie

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Randomly rewatched the 2000 Charlie’s Angels and I was really surprised by how great it was/is. 

The over the top silly action is overlooked because it’s so fun, the soundtrack is great, cliche storyline works so well because the characters and casting. It just hit every note perfectly. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Especially when you're 15
  - You lost yourself
2. That's just the drugs talking
  - Sam Rockwell dancing easily elevates it perfection.
3. Sam Rockwell dancing made it
4. Plus one Bill Murray
5. Lucy Liu in leather!
6. Crazy/scary storyline in 2000/2001: *the bad guys can track anyone's cellphone anywhere in the world*

2025: *this app to play BlockBlast needs your location on at all times*
7. The scene where Cameron Diaz shakes her booty in those Spider-Man undies is so hot 🔥
8. I dunno what ya'll are bitching about, it was a fun movie.

## 4. Bigg Boss 19 Day 32 Highlights: Captaincy Task, Nehal’s Return & Drama

**Score:** 0
**Body:** On Day 32 of *Bigg Boss 19*, the house witnessed drama, fun and emotions. Nehal Chudasama made a shocking comeback, while Awez broke down over allegations. Baseer and Amaal apologized, and Tanya shared her struggles. After three thrilling party tasks, Gaurav Khanna and Farhana Bhatt emerged as the final captaincy contenders, setting the stage for big clashes ahead.

Read Full Bigg Boss 19 Day 32 Highlights: [Bigg Boss 19 Day 32 Highlights: Captaincy Task, Nehal’s Return & Drama](https://possible11.com/article/bigg-boss-19-day-32-highlights-captaincy-task-nehal-return-drama/)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Most underrated movies? I'll go first

**Score:** 0

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Platform was solid from Netflix
2. It did feel pretty dumb that a pampered princess can suddenly become a badass because of plot reasons out of nowhere without any training or experience.

Not a terrible movie but I feel like the target audience was teenage girls who recently had a break-up.
3. Now hold on there. I enjoyed the Enola Holmes movies (silly light Sherlock Holmes riffs with a feminist twist), but saying Damsel is underrated is a step too far.
4. If you ask me, Damsel is INCREDIBLY STUPID, like how come no none of the wealthy families decided "Yo, we invested in marriages and this evil family kills our relatives! LETS GO KILL THEM"? Political marriages were expensive and the news spread around like wildfire. Why won't this whole thing work with peasant girls, if you can taint their blood with royal ones? How is it heroic that our main lady burned the villain's castle WITH ALL THE SERVANTS AND GOOD FOLK INSIDE?

Thats just a tip of the iceberg, but its very forgettable if you ask me
  - I both concur and differ from your statement. The film IS incredibly stupid, and that's precisely what makes it unforgettable (though not in a good way).
5. Kingdom od heaven…
Incredibly underrated in its time… just later to start and gather more and more credits as it should
6. Dude that movie is absolute trash.
7. In about 12 years time the Nutcracker and the Four Realms will be considered a cult Christmas classic.
8. The most underrated movie of all time is Ang Lee's Ride With The Devil (1999)
9. * Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves 
* Out of the Furnace
* Extraction
* Gattaca (usually forgotten when 90’s & 00’s scifi is brought up)

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Is One Battle After Another a critique of state violence? (NO SPOILERS)

**Score:** 63
**Body:** Everyone seems to be talking about  *One Battle After Another* as a stylish action ride, but it also seems like PTA pulled off something pretty subversive here.  Somehow he manages to slip a critique of hypermilitarized society: detention centers, concentration camps, and the normalization of state violence, past the usual censors, while still delivering  us a pretty slick pop-action thriller with chase sequences.  

It would be tricky to make a film about "Christian Nationalists" but "Christmas Adventurers" kind of work as a nice proxy.

As we saw recently, overt critiques of a militarized police state tend to get shut down pretty quick. But by filtering the story through a bumbling clown character (Leo) and the prestige literature of Pynchon, PTA seems to find a way to smuggle a pressing call for a revolutionary & curious mindset into modern public consciousness.  

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved this movie and it was very overtly political. I don’t think the critiques were slipped in as much as they WERE the movie. It’s worth fighting the power for our families, neighbours and freedoms even if it costs us everything. A timely masterpiece by PTA.
2. It’s inspired by “Vineland” by Thomas Pynchon, which is all about that but set in 1960s - 1980s. OBAA is modernized and quite different but thematically the same. So yes. And read “Vineland.”
3. One Battle After Another symbolises (and at the same time quite literally means) the endless struggle for autonomy and emancipation, highlighting the perpetual nature of this struggle and the seemingly hopeless fight of the disenfranchised many against the 'hegemonic one'. The United States hegemony naturally translates to it being the 'hegemonic one' \[state\] dictating the economic, cultural, social, etc. realities of its subjects both domestically and globally.   
  
The Christmas Adventurers is a metaphor showcasing the same logic, an exclusive society aiming to define what is it to be (one with) the 'hegemonic one' that is to be a true citizen = white, male, upper class, catholic etc. The drive to be part of the 'one' a self consuming journey, which according to the film's general approach/angle and protagonist group, leads to a life without any real autonomy, while damaging the autonomy of all. 

Short answer is: partly not primarily, but saying yes would be gravely misleading.
4. After the first act, the leftist revolutionaries stay in the background and their antics early on seem more like thrill-seeking than interest in enacting change. The entire plot hinges on one of the revolutionaries taking fascist dick on the side and ratting out her compatriots. Seeing a stoned DiCaprio watching The Battle of Algiers showed the dichotomy well--he and his compatriots were basically larpers compared to the FLN guerrillas and other 'real' revolutionaries.  

Of course the fascists and supremacists are still worse in every way, but I don't think OBAA portrayed its leftist activists in a very positive light
  - You are shown a whole underground network of 'leftist activists' providing quiet daily aid on the frontlines of the fight throughout the remainder of the movie. The French 75 may have fizzled out but the war is being waged still by people that are portrayed as unambiguously heroic. It ends with Willa, our central focus and the heart of the movie, going to participate in a protest.
    - Completely agree with this read, the entire movie shows a decentralized progressive movement of people actually helping people and deeply involved in community.

Perfidia showed the fallibility of leaders, not of the cause.
  - By contrast Benecio Del Toro’s character was a legit subversive revolutionary - actually helping a sheltering people, quietly, effectively.
5. The efforts to "both sides" the political violence in this film here are deeply confusing and depressing to me.  Much like Eddington, the bothsidesism argument is a superficial one that overlooks or ignores what is actually portrayed.

The fascists are shown as running concentration camps, inciting violence against protestors with false flag attacks, summarily murdering unarmed suspects, and plotting racially motivated child filicide.  Most of this happens without consequence for the perpetrators, except for the guy who gets the "Bedford Forrest" medal of honor.

The revolutionaries are shown committing property crimes, and the one crime resulting in a death is a catastrophe for the group and the person individually responsible in particular.

Only a fascist would say this film portrays leftists negatively.
  - The people who say “both sides bad” in relation to OBAA & Eddington just have poor media literacy
    - Eddington especially. One side protested on the streets and was annoying and whiny and the other side murdered a family in cold blood. How could that possibly be misinterpreted that it was both siding political violence?

## 2. Dangerous Liaisons (1988) and Valmont (1989). One is better and more Dangerous than the other. Both had perfect casting.

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I wish people would talk about Stephen Frears' Dangerous Liaisons (1988) more, which is an amazing and devastating film. But I think it's also a curiosity that Miloš Forman of all directors (having made Amadeus and One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest) made an inferior but still fascinating version of the same story (Valmont, 1989) with the same characters, released less than a year later.

Something crazy is that they shot these films on location in France at the same time, with the cast and crew often interacting with one another.

The casting for both films was incredible, and every actor nailed the role in different ways. Both are worth watching. It's fun to debate whose performance in each was better.

|Character|**Dangerous Liaisons (1988)**|**Valmont (1989)**|
|:-|:-|:-|
|**Merteuil**|Glenn Close|Annette Bening|
|**Valmont**|John Malkovich|Colin Firth|
|**Tourvel**|Michelle Pfeiffer|Meg Tilly|
|**Cécile**|Uma Thurman|Fairuza Balk|
|**Danceny**|Keanu Reeves|Henry Thomas|

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. “Oddity” – Surreal, Disturbing, and Unforgettable!

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Just watched Oddity and I have to say it’s one of the most unique horror films I’ve seen in a long time. The way it blends surreal imagery with psychological terror is honestly genius. The story feels like a nightmare that you can’t wake up from, and the tension keeps building until you’re fully trapped in its eerie world.

What I loved most is how it doesn’t rely on cheap jump scares. Instead, it gets under your skin with its strange atmosphere, haunting visuals, and that constant sense that something isn’t quite right. It’s disturbing in a way that lingers, even after the credits roll.


### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oddity is soooooo good. In a year full of fantastic horror movies, it was the most underrated and one of the best. And although I agree with you that it doesn't rely on cheap jump scares, there is at least one jump scare in it that made me LEAP out of my seat. But in general, yeah — I loved the slow burn of it all, and it really hides its hand well. It's one of those movies where, at any given point, I had absolutely no idea of where it was headed or what was going to happen. I highly recommend to any and all horror fans!
  - The director has some fun horror shorts on his YouTube page, if you haven’t seen them.
  - It's basically a made for TV movie lmao.  Slow burn to no payoff.
    - lmao!
2. My friend recommended it to me randomly one day and I think I legit watched it three times in a row, just back to back. So well done, I'm (clearly) a huge fan of everything about it.
  - That’s awesome when a movie hooks you enough to rewatch it immediately, you know it’s special. It really does have that pull where you notice more layers each time.

## 4. The Brutalist (2024) - Why is Art always conflict?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** One Eisenstein quote came to mind while watching this :

>
"FOR ART IS ALWAYS CONFLICT:
1. because of its social mission.
2. because of its nature,
3. because of its methodology."

Classical, exuberant filmmaking at its finest. Each seductive long take seeps into the other with such confidence that the 3 and three-and-a-half runtime didn't even matter. I was glued to the screen; no one could get me to look away because I won't look away when this kind of precise blocking and movement within the frame occurs. Adrian Brody's bravura performance as a tragic artist trying to wrestle with being an immigrant, an architect, an uncle, a husband, the exploitation of creative force – the explicit meaning is clear here – he represents a filmmaker while Guy Pearce is your typical Hollywood executive – he cares more about his reputation, trying to hide his homosexuality which you do feel that something is being hidden before the reveal happens.

A little deeper amongst the brutalist architecture, an artist successfully immortalises himself within the grand monument which I think looks like King Solomon's First Temple.

It started with an inverted Statue of Liberty, and it ends with an upside-down cross. There are some poetic elements here, Lazlo's memories and dreams, when they're externalised by slow, droning shots. The latter lunch sequence, where slo-mo shots of the other guests are shown in a surreal fashion, like a dream. There is also an element of expectation, Lazlo is told that he's not what they expected, his wife and niece isn't what he had in mind when the narration reads the letter.

It's one of those rare films where the narration actually blends in with the action. Narration is associated with the past, the action (what's happening on the screen) is the present. I read in the Material Ghost :

"The presentness of the action won't tolerate the pastness of narration"

But the narration (past) and action (present) are both synonymously realised here. The past comes roaring back with the urgency of the present.

Finally, down to what exactly keeps the film going – seductive long takes that start from the entrance, then solemnly returns to it. You can make a diagram of the characters' movement. We know it's movement that creates space. There are times when the camera becomes autonomous, focusing not on what's important but something else, like the lunch sequence I mentioned earlier.

From the late 1940s to the 1950s, there is an insertion of documentary footage that lets us know what's happening in the country. It has an ironic effect; the narrator sounds very naive while the situation on the ground is something else.

I think it captures the immigrant assimilation very well, which happens, but it is still incomplete when Americans still treat you like a foreign substance whose desperation to fit in is exploited, even raped, but still the tragic artist in a foreign land triumphs.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Was just reminiscing about watching Past Lives on my plane ride to Japan

Man I loved that movie so much. It did something to me that I couldn’t describe. Beautiful movie that I’m afraid to watch again because of the way it made me feel. Reminds me of the way I felt when watching lost in translation for the first time
